preamble.summary: Enoch recounts his vision: he is carried through clouds and mist into a great house of crystal and fire — the heavenly temple. Beyond it lies an even greater structure, the inner sanctum, where he sees the Great Glory seated on a throne of fire with wheels of flame. Rivers of fire flow from beneath the throne. God speaks directly to Enoch, confirming the rejection of the Watchers' petition.
preamble.remarkable: This is one of the most important throne visions in all of ancient literature. It is a literary precursor to Ezekiel's throne-chariot (Ezekiel 1), Daniel's Ancient of Days (Daniel 7:9-10), and John's heavenly throne room (Revelation 4). The fiery wheels, crystal architecture, and rivers of flame create a template that shaped centuries of Jewish mystical tradition (Merkabah mysticism).
preamble.friction: The relationship between this vision and Ezekiel 1 is debated — some scholars see 1 Enoch 14 as dependent on Ezekiel, others argue both draw on a common Israelite theophanic tradition. The two-room structure (outer house, inner house) mirrors the earthly temple (Holy Place, Most Holy Place), suggesting the heavenly temple as the archetype.
preamble.connections: Ezekiel 1 (throne-chariot vision); Daniel 7:9-10 (Ancient of Days, fiery throne, wheels); Revelation 4 (heavenly throne room); Isaiah 6 (throne vision, temple filled with glory); 1 Kings 22:19 (God seated on throne with heavenly host).

verses.text_reference: The book of the words of righteousness, and of the reprimand of the eternal Watchers in accordance with the command of the Holy Great One in that vision.
verses.rendering: The record of the words of righteousness and of the rebuke of the eternal Watchers, in accordance with the command of the Holy Great One, given in that vision.
translator_notes: This verse serves as a title/header for the vision account that follows, a common literary device in ancient Near Eastern texts.

verses.text_reference: I saw in my sleep what I will now say with a tongue of flesh and with the breath of my mouth: which the Great One has given to men to converse with and understand with the heart.
verses.rendering: I saw in my sleep what I will now declare with a tongue of flesh and with the breath of my mouth — these faculties the Great One has given to people for speech and understanding of the heart.
translator_notes: 'Tongue of flesh' acknowledges the limitation of human language to describe heavenly realities — a humility formula common in visionary literature.

verses.text_reference: As He has created and given to man the power of understanding the word of wisdom, so hath He created me also and given me the power of reprimanding the Watchers, the children of heaven.
verses.rendering: Just as he created and gave to humanity the ability to understand the word of wisdom, so also he created me and gave me the authority to rebuke the Watchers, the sons of heaven.

verses.text_reference: I wrote out your petition, and in my vision it appeared thus, that your petition will not be granted unto you throughout all the days of eternity, and that judgement has been finally passed upon you: yea (your petition) will not be granted unto you.
verses.rendering: I wrote out your petition, but in my vision it was revealed that your petition will not be granted throughout all the days of eternity. Final judgment has been passed upon you — your petition will not be granted.
translator_notes: The definitive denial of the Watchers' petition — repeated for emphasis — is God's final word. There is no appeal, no further intercession possible. This absolute finality distinguishes their fate from human sinners who can repent.

verses.text_reference: And from henceforth you shall not ascend into heaven unto all eternity, and in bonds of the earth the decree has gone forth to bind you for all the days of the world.
verses.rendering: From now on you will not ascend into heaven for all eternity. The decree has gone forth to bind you in the bonds of the earth for all the days of the world.
translator_notes: Permanent expulsion from heaven — the Watchers are forever cut off from their place of origin. This informs Jude 6: 'he has kept in eternal chains, in deepest darkness, for the judgment of the great day.'

verses.text_reference: And (that) previously you shall have seen the destruction of your beloved sons and ye shall have no pleasure in them, but they shall fall before you by the sword.
verses.rendering: Before that, you will witness the destruction of your beloved sons and will take no pleasure in them, for they will fall before you by the sword.

verses.text_reference: And your petition on their behalf shall not be granted, nor yet on your own: even though you weep and pray and speak all the words contained in the writing which I have written.
verses.rendering: Your petition on their behalf will not be granted, nor will your petition on your own behalf — even though you weep and pray and speak all the words contained in the petition I have written.

verses.text_reference: And the vision was shown to me thus: Behold, in the vision clouds invited me and a mist summoned me, and the course of the stars and the lightnings sped and hastened me, and the winds in the vision caused me to fly and lifted me upward, and bore me into heaven.
verses.rendering: And the vision appeared to me in this way: Clouds invited me and a mist summoned me, and the paths of the stars and flashes of lightning hurried me onward. Winds in the vision caused me to fly and lifted me upward and carried me into heaven.
translator_notes: The heavenly ascent via clouds, mist, stars, and wind uses the same motifs found in later merkabah (chariot) mysticism. The passive voice — 'invited me,' 'summoned me,' 'bore me' — emphasizes that Enoch did not achieve this ascent by his own power.

verses.text_reference: And I went in till I drew nigh to a wall which is built of crystals and surrounded by tongues of fire: and it began to affright me.
verses.rendering: I proceeded until I drew near to a wall built of crystal and surrounded by tongues of fire, and it began to terrify me.
translator_notes: The crystal wall with fire is the outer boundary of the heavenly temple. Crystal and fire together — transparent purity and consuming holiness — characterize God's dwelling throughout visionary literature (cf. Ezekiel 1:22, Revelation 4:6).

verses.text_reference: And I went into the tongues of fire and drew nigh to a large house which was built of crystals: and the walls of the house were like a tessellated floor (made) of crystals, and its groundwork was of crystal.
verses.rendering: I entered through the tongues of fire and drew near to a great house built of crystal. The walls of the house were like a mosaic floor made of crystal, and its foundation was crystal.
translator_notes: The first 'house' corresponds to the Holy Place of the earthly temple. The all-crystal construction — walls, floor, foundation — creates an image of total transparency and purity before God.

verses.text_reference: Its ceiling was like the path of the stars and the lightnings, and between them were fiery cherubim, and their heaven was (clear as) water.
verses.rendering: Its ceiling was like the paths of stars and lightning, and between them were fiery cherubim, and their sky was clear as water.
translator_notes: Cherubim in the ceiling directly parallel the cherubim embroidered on the curtains of the tabernacle (Exodus 26:1) and carved in Solomon's temple (1 Kings 6:29). The heavenly temple is the archetype; the earthly temple is the copy.

verses.text_reference: A flaming fire surrounded the walls, and its portals blazed with fire.
verses.rendering: A blazing fire surrounded the walls, and its doorways burned with fire.

verses.text_reference: And I entered into that house, and it was hot as fire and cold as ice: there were no delights of life therein: fear covered me, and trembling got hold upon me.
verses.rendering: I entered that house, and it was hot as fire and cold as ice. There was no comfort of life in it — fear overwhelmed me, and trembling took hold of me.
translator_notes: 'Hot as fire and cold as ice' — the simultaneous extremes of temperature suggest a reality beyond normal physical experience. The paradox conveys the overwhelming otherness of God's presence.

verses.text_reference: And as I quaked and trembled, I fell upon my face.
verses.rendering: As I shook and trembled, I fell on my face.
translator_notes: Falling on the face is the universal response to divine encounter: Ezekiel 1:28, Daniel 8:17, Revelation 1:17. The human body cannot sustain the weight of divine glory.

verses.text_reference: And I beheld a vision, And lo! there was a second house, greater than the former, and the entire portal stood open before me, and it was built of flames of fire.
verses.rendering: And I saw a vision — there was a second house, greater than the first, and its entire entrance stood open before me. It was built of flames of fire.
translator_notes: The second house — the inner sanctum, corresponding to the Most Holy Place — is even greater and more terrifying. It is built entirely of fire, surpassing the crystal-and-fire construction of the outer house.

verses.text_reference: And in every respect it so excelled in splendour and magnificence and extent that I cannot describe to you its splendour and its extent.
verses.rendering: In every way it so surpassed the first in splendor and magnificence and size that I cannot describe to you its glory and its vastness.
translator_notes: The inexpressibility formula — 'I cannot describe' — is a standard feature of ancient visionary literature (cf. 2 Corinthians 12:4, 'heard things that cannot be told'). It functions as both literary device and genuine acknowledgment of human limitation.

verses.text_reference: And its floor was of fire, and above it were lightnings and the path of the stars, and its ceiling also was flaming fire.
verses.rendering: Its floor was fire, and above it were lightning and the paths of stars, and its ceiling also was blazing fire.

verses.text_reference: And I looked and saw therein a lofty throne: its appearance was as crystal, and the wheels thereof as the shining sun, and there was the vision of cherubim.
verses.rendering: I looked and saw a lofty throne. Its appearance was like crystal, and its wheels were like the shining sun, and there was a vision of cherubim.
translator_notes: [TCR Cross-Reference: Ezekiel 1:15-21, Daniel 7:9] The throne with wheels directly parallels Ezekiel's vision of the divine chariot-throne (merkabah) with its wheels (ophannim). Daniel 7:9 also mentions a throne with wheels of burning fire. This shared imagery suggests a common Israelite tradition of the divine throne.

key_terms.note: The wheeled throne connects to Ezekiel's ophannim (1:15-21) and Daniel's fiery throne wheels (7:9). These became central to later Merkabah mysticism.

verses.text_reference: And from underneath the throne came streams of flaming fire so that I could not look thereon.
verses.rendering: And from beneath the throne flowed rivers of blazing fire, so intense that I could not look at them.
translator_notes: Rivers of fire from the throne appear in Daniel 7:10 ('a stream of fire issued and came out from before him'). Fire as the medium of divine presence runs throughout Scripture — the burning bush, Sinai's fire, the pillar of fire.

verses.text_reference: And the Great Glory sat thereon, and His raiment shone more brightly than the sun and was whiter than any snow.
verses.rendering: And the Great Glory sat upon it, and his garment shone more brightly than the sun and was whiter than any snow.
translator_notes: 'The Great Glory' — rather than naming God directly, the text uses a circumlocution emphasizing visible radiance. The description of white garments brighter than the sun reappears in the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:2) and in Revelation 1:14-16.

key_terms.note: A reverential circumlocution for God enthroned. The emphasis is on visible, overwhelming radiance rather than a personal name.

verses.text_reference: None of the angels could enter and could behold His face by reason of the magnificence and glory, and no flesh could behold Him.
verses.rendering: No angel could enter or look upon his face because of the magnificence and glory, and no mortal could behold him.
translator_notes: Even angels cannot look upon God's face — reinforcing Exodus 33:20 ('no one shall see me and live'). The absolute transcendence of God is maintained even in a vision of his throne room.

verses.text_reference: The flaming fire was round about Him, and a great fire stood before Him, and none around could draw nigh Him: ten thousand times ten thousand (stood) before Him, yet He needed no counsellor.
verses.rendering: Blazing fire surrounded him, and a great fire stood before him. No one nearby could approach him. Ten thousand times ten thousand stood before him, yet he needed no counselor.
translator_notes: 'Ten thousand times ten thousand' (one hundred million) appears in Daniel 7:10 and Revelation 5:11. 'He needed no counselor' echoes Isaiah 40:13 and Romans 11:34 — divine sovereignty requires no external input.

verses.text_reference: And the most holy ones who were nigh to Him did not leave by night nor depart from Him.
verses.rendering: And the most holy ones nearest to him did not leave by night nor depart from him.
translator_notes: The perpetual attendance of the holy ones echoes Isaiah 6:2 (seraphim attending the throne) and anticipates Revelation 4:8 ('day and night they never cease to say, Holy, holy, holy').

verses.text_reference: And until then I had been prostrate on my face, trembling: and the Lord called me with His own mouth, and said to me: 'Come hither, Enoch, and hear my word.'
verses.rendering: Until that moment I had been lying face down, trembling. Then the Lord called me with his own mouth and said to me: 'Come here, Enoch, and hear my word.'
translator_notes: God calling Enoch by name and inviting him closer — while no angel can approach — is an extraordinary act of grace. The same pattern occurs with Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:4) and Samuel in the temple (1 Samuel 3:4, 10).

verses.text_reference: And one of the holy ones came to me and waked me, and He raised me up and brought me to the door: and I bowed my face downwards.
verses.rendering: And one of the holy ones came to me and roused me and raised me up and brought me to the entrance. I bowed my face downward.
translator_notes: Being raised by an angel after collapse echoes Daniel 8:18, 10:10, 18. The pattern — fall, angelic assistance, approach — becomes standard in apocalyptic literature.
